strands.title: Replay Module Requirements & Design Intent
strands.content: The replay module (src/pichay/replay.py) must reconstruct offline simulations of context paging behavior from proxy JSONL logs. Core purpose: enable post-hoc evaluation of paging effectiveness by replaying exact message array states at each API call turn, applying compaction logic to detect page faults (model re-requesting evicted content), and computing aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction percentage). This bridges proxy logs to pager compaction logic. User mandate: 'Read the existing pager.py, proxy.py, and eval.py thoroughly before writing any code. Match the coding style and patterns.' The module must have a CLI entry point with argparse supporting --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags. Page faults occur when model's next action re-requests content that was evicted by prior paging operations. The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics. The project is named 'Pichay' (Quechua: 'to sweep, to clean'), explicitly designed to measure the effect of removing dead tool outputs from agentic LLM context windows. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT RESOLVED (cycle 5): The proxy logs currently store only message metrics, not the full messages array. For replay to reconstruct and compact messages, the full message content must be added to request records in proxy.py. This is a prerequisite architectural change—replay.py cannot fulfill its core requirement without this proxy modification.

key_claims.text: The replay module must parse proxy JSONL logs to reconstruct paging scenarios
The module must reconstruct the messages array as it would have existed at each API call turn
Page faults occur when the model's actual next action re-requests content that was evicted
The module must provide per-session and aggregate statistics (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction %)
A CLI entry point with argparse is required with --age-threshold, --min-size, and --json flags
Pichay is a Quechua term meaning 'to sweep, to clean', capturing the project's purpose of removing dead tool outputs
The current proxy logs do not include the full messages array; this gap was confirmed in turn 25
Proxy.py must be modified to log the full messages array before replay.py can be fully implemented

strands.title: Proxy Log Schema — Partially Resolved, Inference Validated
strands.content: The JSONL schema revealed in turn 17 showed: type, timestamp (ISO-8601), model, max_tokens, stream, and system dict containing system_prompt_bytes and system_prompt_typ[...]. The record was truncated mid-field, preventing visibility of deeper structure. The breakthrough in turn 25 represents INFERENCE VALIDATION: the assistant's turn 18 hypothesis that 'proxy log stores metrics about messages but not the full messages array' has now been treated as confirmed. The assistant's statement 'The proxy log currently stores only message metrics, not the full messages array' is presented as established fact rather than hypothesis. This represents a shift from uncertainty (turn 18, indeterminacy 0.2) to higher confidence (turn 25 assertion), though the underlying evidence remains the same truncated schema plus logical inference. The schema itself remains incomplete—we still don't know the full structure of the system dict, whether it contains message count/bytes rather than the full array, or what other top-level fields exist. However, the pragmatic conclusion is now clear: to proceed with replay, the messages array must be added to the request record in proxy.py.

key_claims.text: Proxy JSONL records contain at minimum: type, timestamp (ISO-8601), model, max_tokens, stream, system dict
The system dict contains system_prompt_bytes and a field starting with 'system_prompt_typ' (likely 'system_prompt_type')
The current proxy logs store only message metrics, not the full messages array
Full JSONL record structure beyond the truncated point remains unknown

strands.title: Architectural Pivot: Proxy Extension Before Replay Implementation
strands.content: Cycle 5 marks a decisive shift in project architecture. The original plan was to implement replay.py as specified by the user. However, the discovery (via inference in turn 18, confirmed in turn 25) that the proxy logs lack the full messages array has forced a prerequisite: proxy.py must be extended to log the complete messages array in each request record. This is not optional—without it, replay cannot detect page faults or reconstruct the exact message states that paging would affect. The assistant's turn 25 statement 'I need to add the full messages to the request record' and 'Let me make both changes' indicates a plan to modify proxy.py AND relay.py in parallel. This represents scope expansion: the user did not explicitly request proxy.py modification, but the assistant has inferred it as necessary. The TodoWrite operation (turn 23-24) likely captured this architectural decision. This pivot is epistemically justified (logic is sound: paging affects messages → fault detection requires knowing messages → if messages aren't logged, logs must be extended), but it changes the delivery scope and should be made explicit to the user. The next immediate task is to modify proxy.py to capture the full messages array in the request record.

key_claims.text: The absence of full messages in proxy logs is a blocking constraint for replay implementation
Proxy.py modification is the identified prerequisite before replay.py can be meaningfully implemented
The assistant has decided to modify both proxy.py and replay.py in this cycle
This scope expansion was inferred by the assistant, not explicitly requested by the user

strands.title: Critical Implementation Unknowns — Filtered by New Priority
strands.content: Many unknowns persist from prior cycles, but the emergence of the proxy logging gap has created a new priority hierarchy. PRIMARY UNKNOWNS (now blocking): (1) How to capture the messages array in proxy.py without breaking existing functionality or introducing unbounded data growth. (2) Whether messages should be logged as-is or in a compressed/summarized form (full logging could inflate log size significantly). SECONDARY UNKNOWNS (still relevant but deprioritized): (3) pager.py API (function signatures, invocation patterns). (4) Exact page fault detection method. (5) Metrics calculation (fault rate, bytes saved, reduction %). (6) eval.py patterns. The focus has shifted from 'understand the existing system' to 'identify and fix the missing piece.' The turn 23-24 TodoWrite operation suggests the assistant is now tracking tasks explicitly, which indicates awareness that the project scope has expanded. The glob search for tests (turn 21-22 empty result) combined with the turn 25 assertion suggests the assistant may be proceeding without test coverage or examples to guide the proxy.py modification—relying instead on code inspection and inference.

key_claims.text: How to capture messages in proxy logs without unbounded data growth is now the primary unknown
Whether messages should be logged in full or compressed form is unresolved
pager.py API and page fault detection method remain secondary unknowns but not currently blocking
The assistant appears to be proceeding without test evidence or examples, relying on inference

strands.title: Cycle 5 State: Inference Promoted to Operational Constraint
strands.content: The transition from cycle 4 to cycle 5 represents a subtle but important epistemic move. In cycle 4, the hypothesis 'proxy logs lack full messages' was explicitly labeled as inference with indeterminacy 0.2 and falsity 0.08—it was epistemically uncertain. In cycle 5, the assistant presents this as established fact: 'The proxy log currently stores only message metrics, not the full messages array.' This is a promotion of inference to operational fact without additional validation. The evidence has not changed—we still have only the truncated schema and the logical inference. However, the pragmatic decision has been made: the assistant will now act AS IF this is true and modify proxy.py accordingly. This is operationally sound (the inference is logically valid), but epistemically it represents a confidence escalation without new evidence. The turn 23-24 TodoWrite operation institutionalizes this decision by capturing it in a task list. The assistant is now committed to the proxy modification path. If this inference proves wrong (e.g., messages ARE already logged somewhere in the schema we didn't see), the effort will have been wasted, but the decision was rational given the information available. This represents healthy epistemic pragmatism: act on the best inference while remaining open to correction.

key_claims.text: The 'proxy lacks messages' inference has been promoted to operational constraint without additional validation
This promotion is epistemically justified pragmatically but represents a confidence escalation without new evidence
The TodoWrite operation institutionalizes the proxy modification decision
If the inference later proves false, the proxy modification effort will be wasted, but the decision was rational given available information

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Remaining epistemic uncertainty about whether proxy logs include messages — treated as resolved
declared_losses.why: In cycle 4, the hypothesis that proxy logs lack full messages had truth:0.72, indeterminacy:0.2, falsity:0.08. In cycle 5, the assistant states this as confirmed fact with no additional evidence presented. The truncated schema that motivated the original inference remains the only evidence. The TodoWrite operation (turn 23-24) locked this assumption into the task planning system. This is authorial_choice to treat inference as sufficient basis for architectural decision.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Consideration of alternative explanations for the schema truncation
declared_losses.why: The truncated schema could indicate: (a) messages truly aren't logged, (b) messages are logged but appeared after the truncation point, (c) the tool's output buffer cut off mid-JSON, (d) a different data representation is used. In cycle 5, only explanation (a) is pursued. Alternative hypotheses that might validate the current schema without proxy modification are not explored. This is a context_pressure constraint combined with authorial_choice—the cost of exploring alternatives is high, so the assistant proceeds with the most likely hypothesis.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: User awareness of scope expansion until proxy.py modification is presented
declared_losses.why: The assistant's turn 25 statement 'Let me make both changes' indicates a plan to modify proxy.py without explicitly asking the user for permission or confirmation. The user's original mandate was 'implement replay.py' — not 'modify proxy.py and replay.py.' The architectural decision to extend proxy.py was inferred by the assistant as necessary, but it expands the agreed scope. This is authorial_choice—the assistant prioritizes architectural correctness over explicit scope confirmation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed understanding of how messages should be logged (full vs. compressed, structure, serialization)
declared_losses.why: The assistant's plan to 'add the full messages to the request record' lacks specification about the format, size implications, or serialization method. Critical design decisions (should messages be logged as-is, or summarized? how to prevent log bloat? what structure in the JSONL?) remain open. This is a practical_constraint—the next cycle will need to resolve these design questions before implementation can proceed.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Test evidence base for validating the proxy modification approach
declared_losses.why: The glob search for test files (turn 21-22) returned empty. The assistant has no test cases to reference, no examples of expected proxy log format, and no test-driven constraints on the messages logging design. Implementation will proceed from code inspection and inference alone. This is a practical_constraint—the tooling search returned no results.
